question_text: Verify the address of the emergency. What is the exact address?
What is the callback number you are calling from?
Tell me exactly what happened.
Is the scene safe? Are there any hazards — needles, chemicals, fumes?
Is s/he conscious?
Is s/he breathing?
Is s/he breathing normally — or is it slow, shallow, or irregular?
What did s/he take or what was s/he exposed to?
How much did s/he take?
When did s/he take it? How long ago?
Was it intentional or accidental?
Has s/he vomited?
Are the pupils very small (pinpoint) or very large (dilated)?
Is the skin pale, blue, or flushed?
Is s/he responsive — can s/he answer questions?
Is s/he confused or agitated?
Is s/he having a seizure?
Is there any chest pain or irregular heartbeat?
Does s/he have a history of substance use or prior overdoses?
Is there Narcan (naloxone) available?
Can you find the pill bottle, container, or packaging? How many are missing?
Are there any other people affected?
Does s/he have any other medical conditions?
ECHO-level: Unconscious, not breathing — suspected opioid overdose
condition: unconscious AND not breathing AND opioid suspected

question_text: ECHO-level: Unconscious, not breathing — unknown substance
DELTA-level: Unconscious but breathing, pinpoint pupils — opioid overdose
condition: unconscious AND breathing AND pinpoint pupils

question_text: DELTA-level: Seizures from overdose
DELTA-level: Intentional ingestion of potentially lethal substance
DELTA-level: Chemical exposure with multiple victims
CHARLIE-level: Conscious but altered, ingested medication, able to give some history
condition: altered AND conscious AND medication ingestion

question_text: CHARLIE-level: Vomiting blood or having GI symptoms after caustic ingestion
BRAVO-level: Conscious and alert, accidental double dose of prescription medication
BRAVO-level: Accidental ingestion in a child, substance identified, child alert
ALPHA-level: Accidental minor ingestion, alert, no symptoms, Poison Control consulted
condition: minor ingestion AND asymptomatic AND Poison Control

question_text: OMEGA-level: Ingestion of non-toxic substance, no symptoms, Poison Control advises home monitoring
Do NOT induce vomiting unless instructed by Poison Control.
If Narcan (naloxone) is available and you suspect opioid overdose: spray one dose into one nostril or inject into the thigh.
If the patient is unconscious but breathing, place in the recovery position on their side.
If the patient stops breathing, begin CPR immediately.
If the patient is conscious, do NOT let them take any more of the substance.
Gather the pill bottles, packaging, or containers and have them ready for the paramedics.
If chemical exposure: move the patient to fresh air if safe to do so. Remove contaminated clothing.
Stay on the line. Help is on the way.
If s/he gets worse, stops breathing, or has a seizure, call us back immediately.
